old_claim.note: Maritime Pilotage provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No direct lighthouse_networks to maritime_pilotage edge remains because pilotage is a navigation practice served by lighthouse beacons, not a construction or operating prerequisite for the lighthouse lineage itself.

source_shape.source_locator: UNESCO describes the Tower of Hercules as a late-first-century AD lighthouse and landmark at La Coruna harbor, testimony to lighthouses in antiquity and an elaborate ancient navigation system.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: UNESCO frames the ancient lighthouse as a navigation aid and landmark, not as something built from maritime pilotage practice.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Pilotage may use lighthouse marks, but the source-backed lighthouse system is defined by towers, light, and harbor landmarks rather than pilots' local route knowledge.

ontology_before: Lighthouse Networks depended directly on Maritime Pilotage, blurring the user/navigation practice with the beacon infrastructure.
ontology_after: Lighthouse Networks depends directly on harbor lighthouses; pilotage remains a separate navigation practice that can use lighthouse aids.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: lighthouse_networks no longer has a direct maritime_pilotage prerequisite.
would_reject_if: The node is later rescoped to lighthouse-assisted pilotage offices rather than lighthouse infrastructure.
A source shows ancient lighthouse systems could not be built or operated without formal maritime pilotage institutions.
A separate pilotage_beacon_operations node is added and intentionally depends on maritime_pilotage.
short_reason: Pilotage is the navigation use context, not the direct lighthouse-system substrate.
